[Coordinates](/wiki/Geographic_coordinate_system "Geographic coordinate system"): [55°58′11″N 3°13′12″W﻿ / ﻿55.9698°N 3.2201°W﻿ / 55.9698; -3.2201](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=Inverleith_Sports_Ground&params=55.9698_N_3.2201_W_)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Rugby stadium

The **Inverleith Sports Ground** is a [rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union") stadium in [Inverleith](/wiki/Inverleith "Inverleith"), [Edinburgh](/wiki/Edinburgh "Edinburgh"), Scotland which is the home ground of amateur club [Stewart's Melville RFC](/wiki/Stewart%27s_Melville_RFC "Stewart's Melville RFC") and was the home ground of the [Scotland national rugby union team](/wiki/Scotland_national_rugby_union_team "Scotland national rugby union team") between 1899 and 1925.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Inverleith_Sports_Ground&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Land at [Inverleith](/wiki/Inverleith "Inverleith"), at that time on the undeveloped northern fringe of [Edinburgh](/wiki/Edinburgh "Edinburgh"), was purchased in 1897 by the [Scottish Rugby Union](/wiki/Scottish_Rugby_Union "Scottish Rugby Union") (then the Scottish Football Union), which had previously played international matches at [Raeburn Place](/wiki/Raeburn_Place "Raeburn Place") about 1 kilometre (0.62 mi) further south towards the city centre; that was primarily a [cricket](/wiki/Cricket_(sport) "Cricket (sport)") venue (although it is still used for rugby today as the home of [Edinburgh Accies](/wiki/Edinburgh_Academical_Football_Club "Edinburgh Academical Football Club")). Thus the organisation became the first of the '[Home Unions](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_the_British_Isles "Rugby union in the British Isles")' to have its own ground.[[1]](#cite_note-RFH-1) The first match at the new ground was played on 18 February 1899 when Scotland were defeated 9–3 by [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland_national_rugby_union_team "Ireland national rugby union team") in the [1899 Home Nations Championship](/wiki/1899_Home_Nations_Championship "1899 Home Nations Championship").[[1]](#cite_note-RFH-1)

International rugby was played at Inverleith until the early 1920s, including the first matches played at home by Scotland against [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand_national_rugby_union_team "New Zealand national rugby union team") in [1905](/wiki/History_of_rugby_union_matches_between_New_Zealand_and_Scotland "History of rugby union matches between New Zealand and Scotland") and [France](/wiki/France_national_rugby_union_team "France national rugby union team") in [1910](/wiki/History_of_rugby_union_matches_between_France_and_Scotland "History of rugby union matches between France and Scotland"). Having assessed the condition of the stadium following [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") (when only minimal maintenance was carried out) and the poor potential for expansion to cope with larger crowds as the sport's popularity grew, the SRU decided to move to a new site and transferred operations to [Murrayfield Stadium](/wiki/Murrayfield_Stadium "Murrayfield Stadium") on the west side of the city.[[1]](#cite_note-RFH-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) The last international at the old ground was a victory over France in the [1925 Five Nations Championship](/wiki/1925_Five_Nations_Championship "1925 Five Nations Championship");[[3]](#cite_note-3) the subsequent win over [England](/wiki/England_national_rugby_union_team "England national rugby union team") two months later that clinched the title and a first [Grand Slam](/wiki/Grand_Slam_(rugby_union) "Grand Slam (rugby union)") was the first match at the new ground.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-bbc14-5)

Some further unofficial Scotland matches were played at Inverleith during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") when Murrayfield was requisitioned for military use.[[6]](#cite_note-Scotsman_28Jan2015-6) The [Scotland women's national rugby union team](/wiki/Scotland_women%27s_national_rugby_union_team "Scotland women's national rugby union team") hosted matches there occasionally in the late 20th and early 21st centuries.

Club rugby was initially played at Inverleith by [Stewart's College FP](/wiki/Stewart%27s_College_FP "Stewart's College FP"), the forerunner to the current [Stewart's Melville RFC](/wiki/Stewart%27s_Melville_RFC "Stewart's Melville RFC") who still play there[[7]](#cite_note-7) operating out of the 1990s clubhouse adjacent to the original grandstand[[8]](#cite_note-8) – the owners, [Stewart's Melville College](/wiki/Stewart%27s_Melville_College "Stewart's Melville College"), have their fields for other sports (such as Hockey and Cricket, as well as an impressive number of rugby pitches) in the surrounding area (they were formed by a merger of [Daniel Stewart's College](/wiki/Daniel_Stewart%27s_College "Daniel Stewart's College") and Melville College, whose own sports grounds at Ferryfield, sold off for housing when the institutions merged, were immediately to the north).

## Location

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Inverleith_Sports_Ground&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Location")]

The stadium is located on [Ferry Road](/wiki/Ferry_Road "Ferry Road") (A902);[[9]](#cite_note-9) [Edinburgh Academy](/wiki/Edinburgh_Academy "Edinburgh Academy")'s junior school is located a short distance to the east – much closer than Stewart's Melville College itself; [Goldenacre](/wiki/Goldenacre_Sports_Ground "Goldenacre Sports Ground"), the home of another rival school club [Heriot's](/wiki/Heriot%27s_Rugby_Club "Heriot's Rugby Club"), is around 1 kilometre (0.62 mi) further east. The site is within the Inverleith [conservation area](/wiki/Conservation_area_(United_Kingdom) "Conservation area (United Kingdom)").[[10]](#cite_note-10) Local amateur rugby club [Inverleith RFC](/wiki/Inverleith_RFC "Inverleith RFC") do not play at the old stadium itself but on the Arboretum Road playing fields immediately to the east.[[11]](#cite_note-11)
